summary: Jeff Atwood advocates for adopting the apprenticeship model from traditional trades like electrical work to train software developers. He cites Rob Walling's argument that the 'listen, watch, do, review' cycle used in trade apprenticeships is superior to traditional classroom training. The combination of theoretical learning alongside practical, real-world coding experience produces better developers. Atwood notes that good interns who balance coursework with real code often become excellent developers. However, he acknowledges that effective mentorship requires matching skill levels appropriately—mentors and apprentices can't be too far apart in ability for the relationship to work.
key_points: Traditional software training fails because it only covers 'listen' and 'watch' while skipping the crucial 'do' and 'review' steps that drive real skill development
The apprenticeship model used by trades like electricians—combining night school theory with daytime hands-on work under a journeyman—could transform software training
Interns who work on real business code while simultaneously learning CS theory often develop into exceptional programmers
Effective mentorship requires appropriate skill-level matching; too large a gap between mentor and mentee makes productive collaboration impossible
Software development should cultivate formal apprentice-journeyman-master relationships rather than operating as a loose confederation of tribes
money_quotes: The 'do and review' is what inspires growth and advances skills.
The beauty of apprenticeship is that it tackles theory and experience in one fell swoop.
The mixture of theory by night and real world coding by day is particularly compelling.
Instead of a loose confederation of tribes, maybe we should be cultivating apprentice, journeyman, master relationships in software development.
I have difficulty mentoring developers who are too far apart from me in skill level. I'm too impatient.

If you're putting football players together on a field to scrimmage, don't mix professional players with high school players. The skill disparity is too great for them to actually play football together.
key_insight: Software development training should adopt the trade apprenticeship model of 'listen, watch, do, review' because hands-on practice with mentor feedback—not passive classroom learning—is what actually builds developer skills.
